verbatim_map.@@verbatim:12:8de929@@:
<script id="csinit" type="text/x-cindyscript">
					f(z):=(z-1)/(z^2+z+1);
			hsvToRGB(h, s, v) := (
				regional(j, p, q, t, f);

				h = (h-floor(h))*6;

				j = floor(h);
				f = h - j;

				p = 1 - s;
				q = 1 - s*f;
				t = 1 - s*(1-f);

				if(j == 0, [1, t, p],
				if(j == 1, [q, 1, p],
				if(j == 2, [p, 1, t],
				if(j == 3, [p, q, 1],
				if(j == 4, [t, p, 1],
				if(j == 5, [1, p, q]))))))*v
			);

			color(z) := ( //what color should be given to a complex number z?
				regional(n, grey1, grey2);
				n = 12;
				z = log(z)/2/pi;

				zfract = n*z - floor(n*z); //value of n*z in C mod Z[i]

				grey1 = im(zfract);
				grey2 = re(zfract);

				hsvToRGB(im(z), 1., .5+.5*re(sqrt(grey1*grey2)))
			);

			n = 5;
			
			lastf = "";
			
			zoom = 1;
			lzoom = zoom;

		</script>
verbatim_map.@@verbatim:13:91d8f0@@:
<script id="csdraw" type="text/x-cindyscript">

		//if(Input.text!=lastf & Input.text!="",
		//	parse("f(z) :=" + Input.text);
		//	lastf = Input.text;
		//);
        
		factorial(n) := if(n==0, 1, n*factorial(n-1));
		
		coeff = autodiff(f(z), [complex(A*zoom)], n)_1;
		forall(0..n, k, coeff_(k+1)=coeff_(k+1)/factorial(k));
		
		t(z) := coeff * apply(0..n, k, (z-complex(A*zoom))^k);
		
		colorplot(
			z = zoom*complex(#);
			color(t(z));
			,
			(-2,-2),(2,2)
		);
		
		colorplot(
			z = zoom*(complex(#)+4);
			color(f(z));
			,
			(-6,-2),(-2,2)
		);
		
		//drawtext((-5.9,1.7), "$f(z) =$");
        
        //Draw background for z_0
        shape1 = polygon([(-2,1.7), (-0.25,1.7), (-0.25,2), (-2,2)]);
        fill(shape1,color->(0.8,0.8,0.8));
        draw(shape1, color->(0.8,0.8,0.8));

		// Draw info of z_0
		drawtext((-1.95,1.8), "$z_0=(" + round(zoom * A.x  * 100)/100 + " , " + round( zoom * A.y * 100)/100 + ")$", color->(0,0,0));
        

		//Draw  background for n
		shape2 = polygon([(1.35,2), (2,2), (2,1.75), (1.35,1.75)]);
        fill(shape2,color->(0.8,0.8,0.8));
        draw(shape2, color->(0.8,0.8,0.8));

		// Slider n
        linesize(1.2);
		draw((1.8,-1.5),(1.8,1.65), color->[0,0,0]);
		N.x = 1.8;
		n = round(15*(N.y+1.65));
		n = max(1, n);
		N.y = n/15-1.65;

		drawtext((1.4,1.82),"$n = " + n + "$", color->(0,0,0));
        
        //drawtext((-5.9,1.7), "$f(z) =$", color->(0.98,0.98,0.98));
        //\textcolor{<color>}{<text>}
        //drawtext((-1.98,1.75), "$\sum_{k=0}^{"+n+"} a_k (z-z_0)^k$", color->(0,0,0));
		
		// Line to divide Graphic views
		linesize(1.2);
		draw((-2,-3),(-2,3), color->[0,0,0]);
		
		// Draw text for Zoom
        drawtext((A.x+0.06,A.y+0.06),"$z_0$", color->(0.98,0.98,0.98));
		drawtext((-5.55,-1.85), "Zoom$", color->(1,1,1));
		drawtext((-5.54,-1.85), "Zoom$", color->(0,0,0), size->19.0);
        
		// Draw Slider for Zoom 
		draw((-5,-1.8),(1,-1.8), color->[0,0,0]);
		Zoom.y = -1.8;
		Zoom.x = max(Zoom.x, -5);
		Zoom.x = min(Zoom.x, 1);
		
		zoom = exp(.5 * (Zoom.x + 3));
		
		if(lzoom!=zoom,
			A.xy = A.xy * lzoom / zoom;
		);
		lzoom = zoom;
		
		</script>

                                    console.log("*************");

                                    cdy.evokeCS('f(z) := (' + b.value + '); forcerecompile();');
                                }

                            var sel = function (e, b) {
                                document.getElementById("inp").value = b.value;
                                cdy.evokeCS('f(z) := (' + b.value + '); forcerecompile();');
                            }
                        </script>

Taylor series
For real functions

Let @@math:22:a431bc@@ and @@math:23:1ac8aa@@ be and infinitely differentiable function on an interval @@math:24:7000c6@@ containing @@math:25:0dae20@@ Then the one-dimensional Taylor series of @@math:26:486cdd@@ around @@math:27:6341a0@@ is given by
@@math:28:9c5adf@@
which can be written in the most compact form:
@@math:29:f1445c@@

Recall that, in real analysis, Taylor's theorem gives an approximation of a @@math:30:b78c73@@-times differentiable
                    function around a given point by a @@math:31:34d072@@-th order Taylor polynomial.

For example, the best linear approximation for @@math:32:0f18be@@ is
                    @@math:20:38854d@@
                    This linear approximation fits @@math:33:cbefe0@@ with a line through @@math:34:72f35b@@ that matches the slope of @@math:35:09e4b6@@ at
                    @@math:36:6d7782@@

For a better approximation we can add other terms in the expansion. For instance, the best
                        quadratic
                        approximation is
                        @@math:21:d4755b@@

The following applet shows the partial sums of the Taylor series for a given function. Drag the
                    slider to
                    show more terms of the series. Drag the point [[strong]]a[[/strong]] or change the function.
For complex functions

Suppose that a function @@math:37:f5b14d@@ is analytic throughout a disk @@math:38:280c41@@
                        centered at @@math:39:fc6a59@@ and with radius @@math:40:bfb8f3@@ Then @@math:41:50de57@@ has the power series representation
                        @@math:17:edbf9c@@
                        where
                        @@math:18:f9b8bf@@
                        That is, series (\ref{seriefunction}) converges to @@math:42:7caeec@@ when @@math:43:d4a7e6@@ lies in the stated open disk.

Every complex power series (\ref{seriefunction}) has a radius of convergence.
                    Analogous to the concept of an interval of convergence for real
                    power series, a complex power series (\ref{seriefunction}) has a circle of convergence,
                    which is the circle centered at @@math:44:32e323@@ of largest radius @@math:45:636c6b@@
                    for which (\ref{seriefunction}) converges at every point within the circle @@math:46:977545@@
                    A power series converges absolutely at all points @@math:47:28dadb@@ within its circle of convergence,
                    that is, for all @@math:48:b02f19@@ satisfying @@math:49:19820e@@ and diverges at all points @@math:50:154b50@@ exterior to the
                    circle, that is, for all @@math:51:36fc32@@ satisfying @@math:52:460490@@
                    The radius of convergence can be:
@@math:53:2011ab@@ (in which case (\ref{seriefunction}) converges only at its center @@math:54:934d85@@),

@@math:55:b6a0ab@@ a finite positive number (in which case (\ref{seriefunction}) converges at all interior
                        points of the circle @@math:56:e12cad@@ or
@@math:57:4df807@@ (in which case (\ref{seriefunction}) converges for all @@math:58:e5a057@@).

The radius of convergence can be calculated using the ratio test of convergence. For example,
                    if:

@@math:61:afc778@@ the radius of
                        convergence is @@math:62:bfbcbb@@;

@@math:63:055869@@ the radius
                        of convergence is @@math:64:656c0f@@
Dynamic exploration

Use the following applet to explore Taylor series representations
                    and its radius of convergence which depends on the value of @@math:65:7f1cfa@@

On the left side of the applet below, a phase portrait of a complex
                    function is displayed. On the right side, you can see the approximation
                    of the function through it's Taylor polynomials at the blue base point @@math:66:bfc5a2@@
                    The complex function, the base point @@math:67:ab242a@@ the order of the polynomial (vertical slider)
                    and the zoom (horizontal slider) can be modified.

You can also select one function of the following list: [[br]] 
                                <select id="sel" onchange="sel(event, this)" style="width:12em;">
                                    <option value="(z-1)/(z^2+z+1)">(z-1)/(z^2+z+1)</option>
                                    <option value="1/z">1/z</option>
                                    <option value="z^(1/2)">Square root</option>
                                    <option value="log(z)">Logarithm</option>
                                    <option value="sin(z)">Sine</option>
                                    <option value="0-i*log(i*z+(1-z^2)^(1/2))">Arcsine</option>
                                    <option value="sin(z)/cos(z)">Tangent</option>
                                    <option value="i/2*log((i+z)/(i-z))">Arctangent</option>
                                    <option value="exp(z)">Exponential</option>
                                    <option value="(exp(z)-exp(0-z))/2">Hyperbolic sine</option>
                                    <option value="((exp(z)-exp(0-z))/2)/((exp(z)+exp(0-z))/2)">Hyperbolic tangent
                                    </option>
                                </select>
Maclaurin series

A Taylor series with centre @@math:68:79e012@@

For example, the best linear approximation for @@math:32:0f18be@@ is @@math:20:38854d@@ This linear approximation fits @@math:33:cbefe0@@ with a line through @@math:34:72f35b@@ that matches the slope of @@math:35:09e4b6@@ at @@math:36:6d7782@@

Every complex power series (\ref{seriefunction}) has a radius of convergence.   1. @@math:53:2011ab@@ (in which case (\ref{seriefunction}) converges only at its center @@math:54:934d85@@),
  2. @@math:55:b6a0ab@@ a finite positive number (in which case (\ref{seriefunction}) converges at all interior points of the circle @@math:56:e12cad@@ or
